text_blocks.text: On 6 April 1941, German forces, with
Hungarian and Italian assistance, launched
an invasion of Yugoslavia. On 10 April
1941, Slavko Kvaternik proclaimed the
Independent State of Croatia, and Tito
responded by forming a Military Committee
within the Central Committee of the Yugoslav
Communist Party. Attacked from all sides,
the armed forces of the Kingdom of
Yugoslavia quickly crumbled. On 17 April
1941, after King Peter II and other
members of the government fled the country,
the remaining representatives of the government
and military met with the German
officials in Belgrade. They quickly agreed to
end military resistance. On 1 May 1941,
Tito issued a pamphlet calling on the
people to unite in a battle against the
occupation. On 27 June 1941, the Central
Committee of the Communist Party of
Yugoslavia appointed Tito Commander in
Chief of all project national liberation
military forces. On 1 July 1941, the
Comintern sent precise instructions calling for
immediate action.
